burial.plot_notes: Exact location unknown; earliest surviving identifiable gravestone in cemetery dates to 1648 Memorial plaque text: "Thomas Hooker 1586-1647. A leader of the founders in this commonwealth. A preacher of persuasive power. A statesman who based all civil authority on the free consent of the people."

marriages.officiant: Rev. Robert Chaloner, D.D., rector of Amersham (Susanna's aunt's husband)

sources.full: J.R. Hutchinson, 'The Rev. Thomas Hooker—An Alternative Descent,' NY G&B Record 48 (1917), p. 393
Josephine C. Frost, NY G&B Record 64 (Jan 1933), p. 2

narrative_blocks.header: Led 100 settlers from Cambridge to found Hartford
narrative_blocks.body: In the summer of 1636, Hooker led a company of one hundred men, women, and children from Newtown (Cambridge) to found Hartford — a hundred miles through wilderness with no path, guided only by a compass. They drove 160 cattle before them, cleared thickets with axes, built causeways from felled trees, rested in shady groves, and ate wild strawberries. They made the journey in a fortnight.

narrative_blocks.header: His 1638 sermon framed Connecticut's constitution
narrative_blocks.body: On May 31, 1638, preaching before the General Court assembled to frame Connecticut's constitution, Hooker declared: "The foundation of authority is laid in the free consent of the people." The Fundamental Orders of Connecticut, adopted January 14, 1639 — often called the world's first written democratic constitution — grew from this sermon.

narrative_blocks.header: Called the light of the western churches
narrative_blocks.body: Hooker was universally regarded by his contemporaries as the foremost preacher in New England — Cotton Mather called him "the light of the western churches."

narrative_blocks.header: He moderated the Hutchinson trial, then left
narrative_blocks.body: In 1637, Hooker served as one of two moderators over the three-week inquiry into Anne Hutchinson's antinomian doctrines — which culminated in her excommunication and banishment from Massachusetts Bay. Having completed his role, Hooker returned to Hartford before the trial concluded.

narrative_blocks.header: No portrait exists — only his descendants' faces
narrative_blocks.body: There is no known portrait of Thomas Hooker. The statue that stands before the Old State House in Hartford — near the site of the First Meeting House — was sculpted by comparing the likenesses of his descendants.

cross_connections.display_label: was Hooker's deacon at Cambridge; followed to Hartford
neighbored Hooker's Cambridge circle in Hertfordshire
co-moderated the 1637 Hutchinson trial
co-founded Hartford with Hooker
held the Cambridge lot adjoining Hooker's
married Hannah Holyoke, linking both founding families
co-wrote the Fundamental Orders; first Connecticut governor
served as the colony’s treasurer from 1641 until his death

quotes.text: His genius would still haunt all the pulpits. He had great popularity and influence, especially with young ministers, to whom he was an oracle, and their principle library.

Having gone on, with much strength of voice and intention of spirit, about a quarter of an hour, he was at a stand and told the people, that God had deprived him of both his strength and matter, and so went forth and about half an hour after, returned again, and went on to very good purpose about tw
I giue vnto my sonne Samuell, in case the whole library come not to him... the summe of seuenty pounds.
Brother, I am going to receive mercy!
quotes.attribution: Thomas Hooker, deathbed, July 7, 1647 (Cotton Mather, Magnalia Christi Americana)

career.notes: Cared for Mrs. Joanna Drake; 'had his diet and lodging' in Drake household
Silenced by Bishop Laud for nonconformity
After silencing; John Eliot was assistant
Under surveillance by Laud's agents
Congregation had preceded him
Founded with migration from Newtowne
Embarked 'privately' to avoid arrest

education.notes: B.A. 1608; M.A. 1611; Fellow 1611-c.1618 — Entered divinity course; left before B.D.

burial.plot_notes: Exact location unknown; earliest surviving identifiable gravestone in cemetery dates to 1648 Memorial plaque text: "Thomas Hooker 1586-1647. A leader of the founders in this commonwealth. A preacher of persuasive power. A statesman who based all civil authority on the free consent of the people."

marriages.officiant: Rev. Robert Chaloner, D.D., rector of Amersham (Susanna's aunt's husband)

sources.full: J.R. Hutchinson, 'The Rev. Thomas Hooker—An Alternative Descent,' NY G&B Record 48 (1917), p. 393
Josephine C. Frost, NY G&B Record 64 (Jan 1933), p. 2

narrative_blocks.header: Led 100 settlers from Cambridge to found Hartford
narrative_blocks.body: In the summer of 1636, Hooker led a company of one hundred men, women, and children from Newtown (Cambridge) to found Hartford — a hundred miles through wilderness with no path, guided only by a compass. They drove 160 cattle before them, cleared thickets with axes, built causeways from felled trees, rested in shady groves, and ate wild strawberries. They made the journey in a fortnight.

narrative_blocks.header: His 1638 sermon framed Connecticut's constitution
narrative_blocks.body: On May 31, 1638, preaching before the General Court assembled to frame Connecticut's constitution, Hooker declared: "The foundation of authority is laid in the free consent of the people." The Fundamental Orders of Connecticut, adopted January 14, 1639 — often called the world's first written democratic constitution — grew from this sermon.

narrative_blocks.body: Hooker was universally regarded by his contemporaries as the foremost preacher in New England — Cotton Mather called him "the light of the western churches."

narrative_blocks.header: He moderated the Hutchinson trial, then left
narrative_blocks.body: In 1637, Hooker served as one of two moderators over the three-week inquiry into Anne Hutchinson's antinomian doctrines — which culminated in her excommunication and banishment from Massachusetts Bay. Having completed his role, Hooker returned to Hartford before the trial concluded.

narrative_blocks.header: No portrait exists — only his descendants' faces
narrative_blocks.body: There is no known portrait of Thomas Hooker. The statue that stands before the Old State House in Hartford — near the site of the First Meeting House — was sculpted by comparing the likenesses of his descendants.

Having gone on, with much strength of voice and intention of spirit, about a quarter of an hour, he was at a stand and told the people, that God had deprived him of both his strength and matter, and so went forth and about half an hour after, returned again, and went on to very good purpose about tw

education.notes: B.A. 1608; M.A. 1611; Fellow 1611-c.1618 — Entered divinity course; left before B.D.

narrative_blocks.header: They met in the same household in Surrey
narrative_blocks.body: Susanna Garbrand was working as a waiting-gentlewoman to Mrs. Joanna Drake at Esher, Surrey, when Thomas Hooker arrived in 1620 as curate of Esher church — "having his diet and lodging" in the Drake household. They married at Amersham on April 3, 1621, the ceremony performed by Susanna's own uncle by marriage, Rev. Robert Chaloner.

narrative_blocks.header: Granddaughter of Oxford's Reformation bookseller
narrative_blocks.body: Susanna descended from the Garbrands, a Calvinist family who fled the Low Countries for England. Her grandfather Garbrand Harks became the foremost Oxford bookseller of the Reformation and official stationer to the University, a post the family held for three generations.

narrative_blocks.header: Crossed on the Griffin in the great migration
narrative_blocks.body: In 1633 Susanna sailed to New England aboard the Griffin with her husband and children, part of the Puritan migration that brought some twenty thousand settlers before 1640. The family settled first at Cambridge, then helped found Hartford in 1636.

narrative_blocks.header: Remarried Elder William Goodwin after Hooker's death
narrative_blocks.body: After Thomas Hooker died in 1647, Susanna married Elder William Goodwin, a leading Hartford churchman. In 1654 a Hartford court punished Walter Gray for slandering the couple with a charge of adultery.

narrative_blocks.header: Outlived Hooker by nearly thirty years
narrative_blocks.body: Susanna outlived Thomas Hooker by almost thirty years, dying at Farmington in 1676 at about eighty-three. Through her eight children the Hooker line descended to figures including the financier J.P. Morgan and President William Howard Taft.

narrative_blocks.header: Died 26 days after her last son born
narrative_blocks.body: Her third child John was born April 2, 1646. She died April 28, 1646 — almost certainly of childbed fever, at roughly 24. Her husband Thomas Shepard had already buried his first wife Margaret weeks after arriving in New England.

narrative_blocks.header: Sailed to New England with Thomas Hooker, 1633
narrative_blocks.body: She was born in England around 1621 and crossed the Atlantic with her father on the Griffin in 1633, at roughly age 12. When Thomas Hooker led his congregation to found Hartford in 1636, Joanna remained behind — having married Thomas Shepard the year before.

narrative_blocks.header: Named after Mrs. Drake — her mother's employer
narrative_blocks.body: Her mother Susanna Garbrand had been woman-in-waiting to Mrs. Joan Drake of Esher, Surrey — the woman whose spiritual crisis under Thomas Hooker's pastoral care became the model for his entire theology of conversion. Joanna was born in 1621, the year of her parents' marriage.

narrative_blocks.header: Her son Samuel was raised by his grandfather
narrative_blocks.body: Her firstborn Samuel Shepard was brought up in Thomas Hooker's Hartford household after her early death. The arrangement preserved Joanna's son for his Hooker grandfather while Thomas Shepard continued his Cambridge ministry.

parents.notes: Son of William Shepard of Towcester, England

notable.notable_blurb: Harvard co-founder; key figure in Antinomian Controversy

narrative_blocks.header: Harvard came to Cambridge because of his ministry
narrative_blocks.body: A plaque at Harvard University, quoting Cotton Mather, records that it was "in consideration of the salutary effect of Shepard's ministry" that Harvard College came to be placed at Newtowne (now Cambridge). Harvard was founded in 1636 — the same year Shepard settled there after Hooker's congregation removed to Hartford.

narrative_blocks.header: Took Hooker's pulpit — and bought Hooker's house
narrative_blocks.body: When Thomas Hooker led his congregation from Newtowne to Hartford in 1636, Shepard took over as minister of First Church Cambridge and purchased Hooker's Newtowne property. He was the third minister at the church — succeeding the man whose daughter he would later marry.

narrative_blocks.header: Anne Hutchinson's most vocal clerical critic at trial
narrative_blocks.body: During the Antinomian Controversy (1637-1638), Shepard sat with the colonial ministers at both the civil and church trials of Anne Hutchinson. He was among her most vocal critics during the church trial, which ended in her excommunication. The controversy turned on whether salvation required evidence of sanctification — a question central to Shepard's own pastoral method.

narrative_blocks.header: Margaret died weeks after landing — of consumption
narrative_blocks.body: His first wife Margaret Touteville sailed with him and their infant son on the Defence, arriving at Boston on October 3, 1635. She died of consumption at Cambridge in February 1636 — weeks after landing. Shepard recorded her death and his grief in his autobiography.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at 43 of a peritonsillar abscess
narrative_blocks.body: He died at Cambridge on August 25, 1649, age 43, of quinsy — a peritonsillar abscess, a complication of tonsillitis. He had survived the deaths of two wives and several children. He left an autobiography, published posthumously, that remains one of the most personal records of Puritan spiritual life in colonial New England.

institutions.institution_blurb: Instrumental in founding Harvard College and in placing it at Cambridge, Massachusetts. A plaque at Harvard credits his ministry.

cross_connections.display_label: Both prosecuted Hutchinson — Shepard's voice, Wilson's verdict.
co-moderated the 1637 Hutchinson trial

sources.notes: Graduation year 1655 corrected to 1658 per gravestone and ordination records

sources.label: Sibley's Harvard Graduates, Vol. III, pp. 342-3

narrative_blocks.header: Thomas Hooker raised him for Harvard
narrative_blocks.body: After his mother Joanna's death in 1646, he was brought up in his grandfather Rev. Thomas Hooker's Hartford household. Thomas fitted him for Harvard — making Samuel one of the very few people who knew the founder of Hartford personally.

narrative_blocks.header: Harvard at 17 — Rowley's minister at 24
narrative_blocks.body: He received his B.A. and A.M. from Harvard in 1658 at roughly age seventeen, then was ordained pastor of First Church Rowley on November 15, 1665 — the third minister of that congregation. He served for less than three years before his death.

narrative_blocks.header: Both died within two months — probable epidemic
narrative_blocks.body: His wife Dorothy died February 12, 1668, and he followed fifty-five days later on April 7. Their infant son Samuel was eight months old — two young ministers dying weeks apart in the same town strongly suggests an epidemic.

narrative_blocks.header: Wrote his will — died three days later
narrative_blocks.body: He wrote his will on April 4, 1668; he died on April 7. He left his infant son Samuel — eight months old — to the care of Rev. Henry and Margery Flint of Braintree, with 100 acres at Rowley to come to the boy at age 21.

narrative_blocks.header: Matthew Day's will called him 'little Samuel'
narrative_blocks.body: Matthew Day — the Cambridge printer and Harvard figure — left him a silver spoon engraved with his own name in his will of May 10, 1649, addressing him as 'my old acquaintance little Samuel Shepard.' He was seven years old.

quotes.text: A very Precious, Holy, Meditating, Able and Choice Young Man. His Attainments in Communion with God, and in Daily Meditation and Close Walking, may shame those that are Elder than he. He was but Twenty six years of Age in October last. He was an Excellent Preacher, most dearly Beloved at Rowly, and of all that knew him; but just settled among them. The People would have Plucked out their Eyes for him, to have saved his Life.
quotes.attribution: Rev. Jonathan Mitchell, Harvard 1647, on Samuel Shepard

narrative_blocks.header: Lived three months — first child named John
narrative_blocks.body: Born March 9, 1643 in Cambridge; died July 14, 1643 — three months and five days old. His parents Thomas Shepard and Joanna Hooker named their next surviving son John as well (, b. 1646), as was common when a child died young.

narrative_blocks.header: Born 26 days before his mother's death
narrative_blocks.body: His mother Joanna Hooker Shepard died on April 28, 1646 — twenty-six days after his April 2 birth, almost certainly of childbed fever. Edward Hooker's 1909 genealogy notes only that John 'is supposed to have lived at Lynn, Mass.'

burial.plot_notes: Buried in Peter Prudden's Garden — no tombstone remains

cross_connections.display_label: Her daughter Sarah Newton married her first cousin John Wilson.

narrative_blocks.header: Cared for her mother on the Hartford migration
narrative_blocks.body: According to the Newton family genealogy, Mary was the daughter responsible for caring for her mother Susanna on the arduous journey from Cambridge to Hartford in 1636 — a journey in which Mrs. Hooker 'was carried on a horse litter' in poor health. Mary was roughly 13 years old.

narrative_blocks.header: Stayed in Hartford while Newton built Farmington
narrative_blocks.body: When Roger Newton went to establish the church at Farmington in the late 1640s, Mary remained in Hartford — their son Samuel's baptism on October 20, 1646 appears on the Hartford register, placing her there while her husband laid the groundwork for the congregation he would formally organize.

narrative_blocks.header: Eight children born across three Connecticut towns
narrative_blocks.body: The family's migrations are recorded in their children's birthplaces: Samuel and Roger Jr. born in Hartford; Susannah and John in Farmington; Ezekiel back in Hartford; then Sarah, Mary, and Alice in Milford — where the family finally settled and where Mary died on February 4, 1676.

narrative_blocks.header: Probably born in Esher near Hooker's curacy
narrative_blocks.body: Newton family genealogy says she was born around 1623, probably at Esher, Surrey — where Thomas Hooker had served as curate from c. 1618 to 1625. Esher was also where Hooker met her mother Susanna Garbrand.

parents.father_research_notes: Said to be of the family of Sir Isaac Newton; possibly son of a Samuel Newton. Parentage unproven — see research_notes.

sources.full: Caroline Gaylord Newton, Rev. Roger Newton, Deceased 1683, and One Line of his Descendants (Durham, Conn., 1914), pp. 3-10
Ermina Newton Leonard, Newton Genealogy (Salem, Mass: Higginson Book Company, 1915), p. 700
Mabel S. Hulburt, Farmington Church and Town (Stonington, Conn: The Pequot Press, 1967), p. 8
William Richard Cutter, New England Families, Genealogical and Memorial (New York: Lewis Historical Publishing, 1913), vol. 3, p. 1730

notable.notable_blurb: Founding Farmington pastor; trained Yale's first rector

narrative_blocks.header: Hooker trained him; he founded Farmington's church
narrative_blocks.body: Roger Newton studied theology at Hartford under Thomas Hooker and, around 1645, married Hooker's eldest daughter, Mary. Ordained the first pastor of Farmington in 1652, he later served twenty-three years as the second pastor of Milford.

narrative_blocks.header: He trained Yale's first rector at his table
narrative_blocks.body: As Hooker had trained him, Newton took young men into his Milford home for theological study. One was Abraham Pierson Jr., who became the first rector of Yale College.

narrative_blocks.header: He sheltered the fugitive regicides near Milford
narrative_blocks.body: From 1661 the regicide judges Edward Whalley and William Goffe — hunted for signing the death warrant of Charles I — hid in a cellar near Newton in Milford. Their presence was kept secret through the two years they remained.

narrative_blocks.header: He brought Tunxis members into the church
narrative_blocks.body: Newton did missionary work among the Tunxis and witnessed the 1650 agreement between Connecticut and the tribe. A few Tunxis became members of his Farmington church, with the standing that membership carried.

narrative_blocks.header: He left Farmington in a year of calamity
narrative_blocks.body: Newton departed Farmington in 1657 after a season of disaster — a man named Scott was murdered, and the house of John Hart burned with most of the family inside. He waited months in Boston for passage to England before settling instead at Milford.

cross_connections.display_label: was received into Milford Church by Newton, 1661
also sheltered the regicides, at Guilford
trained by Newton; became Yale's first rector
succeeded Newton as Farmington's pastor

education.notes: Studied at Harvard but name not on graduate list. Some early Harvard records in which his name would naturally appear were accidentally burned; uncertain he received a diploma. Cotton Mather nevertheless speaks of him as one of the young students who came from England to finish their education in America.

bio.title: Reverend
bio.bio_blurb: First pastor of Farmington; second of Milford

career.notes: Ordained the church's first pastor, October 13, 1652.
Second pastor; installed August 22, 1660.

burial.plot_notes: Buried in Founders' Cemetery (Rev. Peter Prudden's Garden) on the Wepawaug; 1889 memorial at Founders Memorial Bridge, Milford.
Oldest part of the Milford Cemetery. FindAGrave #29848117.

cross_connections.display_label: Their wives were half-sisters — Fenn family.

narrative_blocks.header: Militia ensign in King Philip's War
narrative_blocks.body: When King Philip's War broke out in 1675, Samuel was appointed ensign in the Milford militia at roughly age 29. He served through the war and its recurring aftermath of Indian skirmishes, rising to captain of the Milford militia by 1698.

narrative_blocks.header: Represented Milford fifteen times in the General Court
narrative_blocks.body: He represented Milford in fifteen sessions of the Connecticut General Court between 1691 and 1703 — a span of sustained civic service that ran alongside his captaincy of the militia. His father Roger Newton Sr. had helped found the town's church.

narrative_blocks.header: Thomas Hooker left him £10 in his will
narrative_blocks.body: He was nine months old when his grandfather Rev. Thomas Hooker died in 1647. Thomas's will left a £10 legacy to him — a substantial inheritance for a Hooker grandchild born during the Hartford founding.

narrative_blocks.header: His wife and cousin's wife were Fenn half-sisters
narrative_blocks.body: His wife Martha Fenn's half-sister Susanna married William Hooker — Samuel's first cousin. The Fenn family thus connected to the Hooker tree twice in the same generation through different lines.

narrative_blocks.header: A second marriage to a soldier's widow
narrative_blocks.body: After Martha Fenn died, Samuel married Sarah Welch Fowler, widow of John Fowler, who had been killed taking an Indian fort in 1693. Their son Abner bore a name found nowhere else in the Newton or Hooker line.

career.notes: Represented Milford in 15 sessions of the General Court, 1691-1703.

military_service.notes: Appointed ensign 1675 at outbreak of King Philip's War. Served in recurring skirmishes with Indians. Rose to captain of Milford militia 1698. Source: Newton Genealogy (Leonard 1915).

narrative_blocks.header: Father's silk cap — used at Fletcher's baptism
narrative_blocks.body: His father Rev. Roger Newton's 1683 will bequeathed him a silk cap 'for a child' — written before any child was born. The cap was used approximately eighteen months later at the baptism of his son Fletcher on January 15, 1685. The will anticipated a family that had not yet arrived.

narrative_blocks.header: Wife died the day before him
narrative_blocks.body: Abigail died April 18, 1690 in Milford; Roger died April 19 — one day later, aged about 42. Both are buried in the Milford Cemetery. Their children Fletcher and Mary were five years old and not yet two.

narrative_blocks.header: Brother and husband both married Fenn sisters
narrative_blocks.body: Her brother Samuel Newton married Martha Fenn in 1669. Her second husband Samuel Burwell had previously been married to Sarah Fenn — Martha's sister, same parents. Two Newton siblings married into the same Fenn family, though into different branches of it.

narrative_blocks.header: Father left her his three hour-glasses
narrative_blocks.body: Because Susannah had already received her portion at marriage, Roger Newton Sr.'s 1683 will left her only one cow and all three of his hour-glasses. She was living in Guilford with five children at the time. The genealogical note adds dryly: 'No one can tell why she should have all the hour-glasses.'

narrative_blocks.header: Two marriages, six children, two Connecticut towns
narrative_blocks.body: She had four children with her first husband John Stone, then married Samuel Burwell as his second wife. She was living in Guilford with five children when her father's 1683 will was written, then moved to Milford with the Burwells. She died at Milford in July 1703, age 48.

marriages.officiant: Major Robert Treat, Deputy Governor of Connecticut

narrative_blocks.header: Married by Connecticut's future governor in 1680
narrative_blocks.body: His marriage to Lydia Ford on April 14, 1680 in Milford was performed by Major Robert Treat, then Deputy Governor of Connecticut. Treat would become Governor in 1683 and remain in office until 1698 — the same year John Newton died.

narrative_blocks.header: Built his house next to his father's
narrative_blocks.body: He built his house immediately adjacent to Roger Newton Sr.'s on the same Milford home lot. His daughter Prudence — eighteen months old — was 'just old enough to run across the yard to her grandfather's' during Roger's final illness in early 1683.

narrative_blocks.header: May 1699 deposition: widow Lydia and four children
narrative_blocks.body: He died in May 1699 in Milford. Lydia's deposition for the May 18 estate inventory survives, listing their children — Prudence about 18, Thomas about 17, Ezekiel, and John about 2. She was 38 and a widow with four to raise.

narrative_blocks.header: Lydia Ford lived 48 years a widow
narrative_blocks.body: Lydia Ford remained a widow until her death in 1747 at age 86 — 48 years after John's death. Milford Land Records show numerous transactions in her name, and her 1746 will divided the estate among her surviving children and grandchildren.

sources.full: Nathan Pond, Inscriptions on Tombstones in Milford, Conn., Erected Prior to 1800 (New Haven, 1889), p. 43, footnote 287
Connecticut State Library (Hartford), Probate Packets, New Haven, 1683-1880

narrative_blocks.header: Milford's physician, who never married
narrative_blocks.body: Ezekiel Newton practiced as a physician at Milford and never married. He died there in 1734 at seventy-four, and was buried at Milford Cemetery.

narrative_blocks.header: A coat for a child he never had
narrative_blocks.body: His father’s 1683 will left him a yellow say-coat “for a child” — read as provision for a marriage that never came. He kept half the family house, the other half going to his unmarried sisters, and the Milford physician died single at seventy-four.

narrative_blocks.header: His whole estate fit on one page
narrative_blocks.body: The 1734 inventory of everything he owned was brief: clothing, a bed, a few animals and tools, and two books — one titled “Ye Felicity of Man.” A modest close for the son of Milford’s second minister.

cross_connections.display_label: She and her first cousin John Wilson married — Hooker descendants.

narrative_blocks.header: Married her first cousin — both Hooker grandchildren
narrative_blocks.body: Her husband John Wilson was her first cousin: her mother was Mary Hooker, his mother was Sarah Hooker — sisters, both daughters of Rev. Thomas Hooker. They married July 4, 1683 in Milford. EH and the Wilson family genealogy both confirm the cousin relationship explicitly.

narrative_blocks.header: Married 27 days after her father's death
narrative_blocks.body: Her father Roger Newton Sr. died June 7, 1683. She married John Wilson on July 4, 1683 — twenty-seven days later. The marriage had presumably been planned before his death, but she wed her cousin in the immediate shadow of her father's loss.

narrative_blocks.header: Inherited 150 Farmington acres from her father
narrative_blocks.body: Her father Roger Newton Sr.'s 1683 will left her 150 acres in 'Bohemia' Farmington. Family tradition speculates the land may have originally been given to her mother Mary Hooker by Governor Haynes — connecting it to the Hartford founding generation.

narrative_blocks.header: She never married, keeping the family home
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Newton never married. The Newton family record places her still living in 1689, keeping the family home in joint possession with her sister Alice.

narrative_blocks.header: Left £30 for a great-nephew's Yale degree
narrative_blocks.body: Her will of December 1740 left thirty pounds to Roger Newton, son of Abner Newton of Durham, on condition that 'if the said Roger prove likely for public service' and his parents bring him to college: ten pounds on entering, twenty on taking his B.A. She died in 1741 when the boy was about five.

narrative_blocks.header: He entered Yale 18 years after her death
narrative_blocks.body: She died in 1741 when her great-nephew Roger Newton was about five years old. He nonetheless fulfilled her wish — entering Yale, graduating 1758, and serving as minister at Greenfield, Massachusetts until his death. Her conditional bequest reached its purpose.

cross_connections.display_label: Her great-nephew fulfilled her Yale bequest by entering ministry

narrative_blocks.header: Father forbade it — he married and stayed
narrative_blocks.body: Thomas Hooker's will, written hours before his death in 1647, addressed his son's future: he could seek a wife in England, but was forbidden from marrying and tarrying there. John disobeyed on both counts — married in England, and never returned to New England.

narrative_blocks.header: Jovial parson — dissipated son of Thomas Hooker
narrative_blocks.body: Remembered as 'a jovial, rollicking, sporting Parson,' he chose Oxford over his father's Emmanuel College, Cambridge, and was ordained by the Bishop of Oxford into the Church of England in 1660.

narrative_blocks.header: Found dead in bed — buried at midnight
narrative_blocks.body: He died in 1684 — found dead in bed, then buried privately at midnight. The manner of burial suggests a death considered shameful or suspicious; possibly self-inflicted.

narrative_blocks.header: The only Hooker line that ended in England
narrative_blocks.body: He was Thomas Hooker's first son, born around 1626 at Chelmsford. His was the only Hooker line that returned to England — and it ended with him; no verified descendants survive.

narrative_blocks.header: Four daughters; two died young, two lines lost
narrative_blocks.body: John married Mary Lovell at Leckhampstead in 1659, and they had four daughters. Two died in infancy, and the lines of the surviving two cannot be traced.

career.notes: Ordained 6/5/1660 by Bishop of Oxford. Denomination: Church of England.

narrative_blocks.header: Baptized in January, buried by May 1626
narrative_blocks.body: Anne, the third child of Thomas and Susanna Hooker, was baptized at Great Baddow in January 1626 and buried at Chelmsford that May. She died in infancy, before her first birthday.

winthrop_journal.text: Mr. Hooker's young son died of smallpox.

narrative_blocks.header: The eighth child, lost in infancy 1634
narrative_blocks.body: The eighth and last child of Thomas and Susanna Hooker, an unnamed son, died at Cambridge late in 1634.

narrative_blocks.header: Lived to age 95
narrative_blocks.body: Lived to age 95

narrative_blocks.header: Outlived husband by 34 years
narrative_blocks.body: Outlived husband by 34 years

narrative_blocks.header: Her son married her sister Mary's daughter
narrative_blocks.body: Her son John Wilson married Sarah Newton — the daughter of Mary Hooker Newton, Sarah's own sister. Both of Thomas Hooker's daughters had children of the same generation who married each other, making their grandchildren the first documented double-descent in the Hooker tree.

cross_connections.display_label: Her son John Wilson married his first cousin Sarah Newton.
was her father-in-law and Boston’s first minister

parents.father_research_notes: Son of Rev. John Wilson Sr., first minister First Church Boston

narrative_blocks.header: Founded Medfield church — served forty years
narrative_blocks.body: He became the first minister of Medfield, Massachusetts in 1651 and served the congregation for forty years until his death in 1691. He had previously served as a colleague of Rev. Richard Mather at Dorchester, and received his education at Harvard College in 1642.

narrative_blocks.header: Harvard 1642 — the first graduating class
narrative_blocks.body: He attended Harvard College in 1642, effectively the first graduating class of the institution. His father Rev. John Wilson Sr. was a close ally of Rev. Thomas Hooker, and John Jr. married Hooker's daughter Sarah — bringing together two of the founding families of Puritan New England.

narrative_blocks.header: Son of Boston's founding minister
narrative_blocks.body: His father, Rev. John Wilson Sr., was the first minister of the First Church of Boston from its founding in 1630 until his death in 1667 — the man who formally excommunicated Anne Hutchinson in 1638 and who supervised the execution of Mary Dyer in 1660.

narrative_blocks.header: Fell four stories — taken up for dead
narrative_blocks.body: Cotton Mather records that as a child, Wilson "fell upon his head from a loft four stories high into the street; from whence he was taken up for dead, and so battered and bruised and bloody with his fall, that it struck horror into the beholders." His father's prayers were credited with his recovery. He went on to serve Medfield forty years as minister, physician, and schoolmaster.

career.notes: Studied at Eaton's school (introductory to Harvard). Testified to ill-treatment of scholars by Eaton.
Colleague of Rev. Richard Mather at Dorchester. Known as "the gracious and godly Mr. Wilson."
Settled 1651. Ordained pastor Oct 12, 1652. Also served as physician and schoolmaster. Served 40 years until his death.

narrative_blocks.header: John died young
narrative_blocks.body: John died in 1659 at the age of nine. John’s parents gave the name to a child born one year later.

cross_connections.display_label: Her daughter Sarah Batt married Josiah's nephew.
Her daughter Sarah Batt married Josiah's nephew.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost first husband Paul Batt within four years
narrative_blocks.body: She married Paul Batt in 1674; he died in Boston in July 1678, leaving her widowed at twenty-eight. Two years later she married Josiah Torrey of Medfield and bore him three more children.

narrative_blocks.header: Three colonies across two marriages
narrative_blocks.body: Born in Dorchester and raised in Boston, she moved to Medfield with second husband Josiah Torrey, then on to Mansfield, Connecticut. She died in Mansfield around 1725, having lived in three of the New England colonies during her seventy-five years.

narrative_blocks.header: Three founding ministers — father, grandfather, great-grandfather
narrative_blocks.body: Her father Rev. John Wilson Jr. was the first minister of Medfield. His father Rev. John Wilson Sr. was the first minister of the First Church of Boston. Her maternal grandfather Rev. Thomas Hooker was the first minister of Hartford.

cross_connections.notes: Their children carry descent from Thomas Hooker through two separate lines — descent_paths_to_thomas: 2
cross_connections.display_label: John Wilson and his wife Sarah Newton were first cousins.

cross_connections.notes: Their children carry descent from Thomas Hooker through two separate lines — descent_paths_to_thomas: 2
cross_connections.display_label: John Wilson and his wife Sarah Newton were first cousins.

cross_connections.notes: Their children carry descent from Thomas Hooker through two separate lines — descent_paths_to_thomas: 2
cross_connections.display_label: John Wilson and his wife Sarah Newton were first cousins.

institutions.institution_blurb: Left Harvard College soon after admission; educated by his father instead.

narrative_blocks.header: A regicide praised his preaching in 1682
narrative_blocks.body: In 1682 John Wilson preached in New Haven for over a year. Col. John Dixwell — one of the English regicides then living in hiding in New Haven — wrote to Rev. Increase Mather calling him "a veire hopefull yonge man of good abilities." Wilson returned to Medfield in 1684 and eventually abandoned the ministry.

narrative_blocks.header: Left Harvard — his father taught him everything
narrative_blocks.body: He left Harvard College soon after admission, but received a thorough private education from his father in divinity, medicine, and law. He settled in Braintree in 1692 and spent the rest of his life farming, practicing medicine, and performing legal services.

narrative_blocks.header: Married his first cousin — both Hooker grandchildren
narrative_blocks.body: His mother was Sarah Hooker Wilson (Thomas Hooker's daughter); his wife Sarah Newton's mother was Mary Hooker Newton (also Thomas Hooker's daughter). The two mothers were sisters. EH and the Wilson genealogy both explicitly note the cousin relationship.

career.notes: Employed as schoolmaster in Medfield for several terms; also assisted father in ministry.
Representative from Braintree, 1698.
Commission June 7, 1700. Held until death.

military_service.notes: Commission as captain of one of the local military companies, 1696.

burial.inscription: Here lies Inter'd ye Remains of Mrs. Susanna Rawson Relict of ye late Rev'd Mr. Grindall Rawson, Pastor of a Church of Christ in Mendon & Daughter of ye Rev'd Mr. John Wilson 1st Minister of ye Gospel in Medfield who changed mortality for life Aug. 23, 1691. She was born Dec. 1, 1664, & died July 8, 1748, in ye 84th year of her age.

sources.full: Gary Boyd Roberts, Ancestors of American Presidents
sources.label: Roberts 2009

narrative_blocks.header: Wrote Cotton Mather a eulogy for her husband
narrative_blocks.body: After Rev. Grindal Rawson died on February 6, 1715, Susanna wrote a long letter to Cotton Mather — a detailed portrait of her husband's forty-year ministry at Mendon, his Indian mission work, his character, and his final hours. His last words were "Come Lord Jesus, come quickly!" The letter was published in the Rawson Family Memorial. Cotton Mather quoted it in the sermon he preached at Rawson's funeral.

narrative_blocks.header: Her gravestone names her father, not husband
narrative_blocks.body: Her gravestone in Mendon reads: "Here lies inter'd ye Remains of Mrs. Susanna Rawson, Relict of ye late Rev. Mr. Grindal Rawson... and daughter to ye Rev. Mr. John Wilson 1st Minister of ye Gospel in Medfield." She outlived her husband by 33 years, dying July 8, 1748, age 83.

narrative_blocks.header: Ancestor of President Taft through both Taft brothers
narrative_blocks.body: Her granddaughter Mary Dorr Taft married Moses Taft, and her great-granddaughter Rhoda Rawson Taft married Aaron Taft — Moses's and Aaron's fathers were brothers. President William Howard Taft descends through the Aaron Taft line.

cross_connections.display_label: was her father-in-law, the Bay Colony’s secretary

burial.plot_notes: No stone survives. Well-attested tradition places the grave at the south side of Rev. Samuel Whitman's marked grave. "Mr. Whitman like Mr. Hooker, died while the minister of the town, and tradition says he was buried by the side of Mr. Hooker."

sources.notes: John Winthrop Jr., Alchemy and the Creation of New England Culture, 1606-1676 (Homer Babbidge Prize)

narrative_blocks.header: He inherited his father's library — and his calling
narrative_blocks.body: Thomas Hooker's will gave his library to John on condition that John pursue ministry; if John faltered, the entire library and manuscripts went to Samuel. John did not become a minister. Samuel received his father's books — and then dedicated his own life to the ministry his father had hoped for.

narrative_blocks.header: Prosecutor in the Hartford witch trials
narrative_blocks.body: In the 1660s he formed a prosecutorial tribunal with Rev. Samuel Stone of Hartford and Rev. Joseph Haynes of Wethersfield. Connecticut tried witches more aggressively than Massachusetts in this decade — three decades before the Salem trials.

narrative_blocks.header: Ordered to King Philip's War, then quietly replaced
narrative_blocks.body: On March 9, 1675-6, the Council ordered Hooker to prepare to march into King Philip's War with Major Treat's army toward Hadley and Northampton. Two days later, the Council quietly replaced him with Rev. Israel Chauncy. No reason was recorded — only the substitution.

narrative_blocks.header: He persuaded Farmington to found a free school
narrative_blocks.body: In December 1685, the town of Farmington voted to give £30 for a schoolmaster for one year — and declared it a free school for the town, almost certainly at Hooker's instigation. It was among the earliest free public schools in colonial Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: The 'Fervent Hooker,' who preached from the heart
narrative_blocks.body: Rev. Pitkin described him as 'an excellent preacher, his composition good, his address pathetic, warm and engaging.' Hooker told a friend he had three things to do with his sermons before delivering them: write them, commit them to memory, and get them into his heart.

cross_connections.display_label: Five Hooker generations on the same Farmington ground.
Farmington's first pastor, whom Hooker succeeded
His grandfather Tobie Willet sailed with John Talcott on the Lion.
Her husband's Prudden line connects to his wife Mary Willet's family
was his father-in-law; First Mayor of New York City, 1665

quotes.text: Thus we have to this day among us our dead Hooker, yet living in his worthy son Samuel Hooker, an able, faithful, useful minister at Farmington, in the Colony of Connecticut.

career.notes: Succeeded brother-in-law Roger Newton

education.notes: B.A. 1653. Entered Harvard 1651. Chosen Fellow of the College November 27, 1654.

burial.plot_notes: Near center of burying ground, near East Norwalk railway station

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter of New York City's first mayor
narrative_blocks.body: Her father Capt. Thomas Willet was appointed the first Mayor of New York City in 1665, when the English renamed the Dutch colony. He had previously succeeded Capt. Miles Standish as commander of Plymouth's militia — one of the most storied military commands in early New England. Mary carried this distinguished lineage to Farmington when she married Rev. Samuel Hooker.

narrative_blocks.header: Three generations of Willet women known for beauty
narrative_blocks.body: Mary's mother was described as possessing 'beauty and majesty almost divine.' A portrait of Mary's daughter Mary Hooker Pierpont survives, and from it contemporaries formed 'the opinion that she was a woman of uncommon beauty.' Her daughter Sarah was 'accounted the most accomplished lady that had ever come to Norwalk.'

narrative_blocks.header: Remarried at 66 — outlived two minister husbands
narrative_blocks.body: After Samuel Hooker's death in 1697, Mary married Reverend Thomas Buckingham of Saybrook at age 66. When he died in 1709 she moved to Norwalk to live with her son-in-law Reverend Stephen Buckingham, where she remained until her death in 1712 at age 74.

narrative_blocks.header: She and her daughter married father and son
narrative_blocks.body: After Rev. Samuel Hooker died, Mary Willet married Rev. Thomas Buckingham of Saybrook in 1703. Her daughter Sarah was the wife of his son, Rev. Stephen Buckingham, so a mother and daughter had married a father and his son.

cross_connections.display_label: Her grandfather Tobie Willet sailed to New England with John Talcott.
Her father Thomas Willet's widow's first husband was ancestor of H00153's husband

narrative_blocks.header: His wife's grandfather wrote a book Boston burned
narrative_blocks.body: Mary's mother was Ann Pynchon, daughter of William Pynchon — who founded Springfield, Massachusetts, and whose 1650 book The Meritorious Price of Our Redemption became the first book publicly burned in New England. Thomas Hooker — grandson of the founder of Hartford — married the granddaughter of the man whose book the Puritans burned.

narrative_blocks.header: Physician in Hartford, son of Farmington's minister
narrative_blocks.body: He was born in Plymouth, Massachusetts in 1659 and trained as a physician in Hartford. He was the second son of Rev. Samuel Hooker of Farmington and great-grandson of Rev. Thomas Hooker, founder of Hartford.

narrative_blocks.header: Married a widow whose husband drowned at sea
narrative_blocks.body: His wife Mary Smith was the widow of Richard Lord II, lost at sea on November 5, 1685. Thomas married her between February and October 1686 — less than a year after she was widowed.

narrative_blocks.header: He managed his stepson's inheritance for seven years
narrative_blocks.body: When Thomas Hooker married the widow Mary Smith in 1686, he took charge of her first husband's Hartford estate. He administered it until 1693, when control passed to his stepson, young Richard Lord.

career.notes: Physician in Hartford. Also administered estate of Richard Lord II (wife's first husband) from 1686 until released Feb 8, 1693.

sources.notes: Samuel Hooker Jr. biography; confirms Plymouth birth, Hartford merchant activity

landmarks.landmark_blurb: His father lived here, directly opposite the Giles Hooker house on Main Street.

narrative_blocks.header: Born in Plymouth in his father's transition year
narrative_blocks.body: He was born in Plymouth, Massachusetts on May 29, 1661 — where his father Rev. Samuel Hooker had been preaching for several years. The family moved to Farmington that same year, where his father succeeded his brother-in-law Roger Newton as minister.

narrative_blocks.header: His house stood south of the burial ground
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel Hooker II had a fine residence at Farmington, next south of the old burial ground — later known as the Mix house — and ran a store for mercantile business in Hartford. He was one of the first of the Farmington Hookers to move from ministry into trade.

narrative_blocks.header: He married a wealthy sea captain's daughter
narrative_blocks.body: In 1687 Samuel Hooker married Mehitabel Hamlin of Middletown, daughter of Capt. Giles Hamlin, a noted and wealthy sea captain and merchant. Hooker kept a store and traded out of Hartford while living at Farmington.

career.notes: Mercantile business. Store and fine residence at Farmington.

education.notes: B.A. Harvard; later a fellow of the college. (Julius Gay, Farmington Historical Pamphlets)

burial.plot_notes: Ancient Burying Ground, Hartford CT. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

cross_connections.display_label: His wife's sister married the Farmington minister he succeeded.
Their mothers were sisters — the Fenn family ran through both.
His father-in-law — patriarch of the Fenn family.

narrative_blocks.header: Store at Farmington's most prominent corner
narrative_blocks.body: William Hooker's store stood at the most prominent corner in Farmington — opposite his father's house, at the junction of main street and the road over the stone bridge. He died at 26, leaving only a daughter, Susannah, who was named in her grandfather Rev. Samuel Hooker's will.

narrative_blocks.header: Three Fenn sisters married three prominent men
narrative_blocks.body: His wife Susanna Fenn was one of three Fenn sisters who married into prominent Connecticut families in the same generation. Susanna married him; her sister Martha married Samuel Newton; her sister Mary married William Leete Jr.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at twenty-six with one infant daughter
narrative_blocks.body: He died in 1689 at age 26 — his will dated August 8 with inventory totaling £364-11-06. He left wife Susanna and an infant daughter Susannah; Susanna remarried John Blackleach within a few years.

narrative_blocks.header: Son of Rev. Samuel Hooker, grandson of the founder
narrative_blocks.body: William was a son of Rev. Samuel Hooker of Farmington and a grandson of Thomas Hooker. His store stood across the road from his father's house.

narrative_blocks.header: His widow married the merchant John Blackleach
narrative_blocks.body: After William died in 1689, his widow Susanna Fenn married John Blackleach, a noted Hartford merchant. Their infant daughter Susannah carried the Hooker line forward.

career.notes: Store at the most prominent corner in Farmington, opposite his father's house, at the junction of main street and the road over the stone bridge.

sources.full: L.J. Hooker, 'Edward Hooker, Commander, U.S. Navy,' The Old Northwest Genealogical Quarterly (Columbus, Ohio, 1898-), VII, 12

cross_connections.notes: Col. Wadsworth's son Daniel Wadsworth founded the Wadsworth Atheneum in Hartford — a monument with Hooker family kinship interest. Research needed to confirm Mehitable Russell's Hooker descent line. Warren 1887 confirms: Elizabeth Standley married John Wadsworth — their son Daniel Wadsworth founded the Wadsworth Atheneum.
cross_connections.display_label: was his father-in-law, honored in the Indian wars

cross_connections.notes: Capt. Thomas Hart b. ca. 1644, d. Aug 27, 1726 age 82. Son of Stephen Hart (X00034). Brother of John Hart who survived the 1666 fire (X00033). Not in this tree through marriage — noted as civic context only.
cross_connections.display_label: helped govern Farmington alongside him for decades
married a descendant of both Hooker and Standley
served Hartford’s founding-family public life alongside him
was his wife’s sister and Col. Jeremiah Wadsworth’s grandmother
fathered four children who married four of his own
Both served as Connecticut Colony Supreme Court judges.
was the cousin whose Indian Neck deed he registered
signed a Tunxis dispossession deed as his descendant’s in-law
shared his entanglement in Tunxis affairs, decades apart
was called with him one of Farmington’s two prominent men

burial.plot_notes: Memento Mori Cemetery, Farmington, Hartford County, Connecticut. Same cemetery as his father Rev. Samuel Hooker (H00007).

notable.primary_url_label: Native Northeast Portal — John Hooker biography

narrative_blocks.header: Judge of Connecticut Colony's Supreme Court
narrative_blocks.body: He served as Judge of the Supreme Court of Connecticut Colony from 1724 to 1732 and as Speaker of the Lower House. He also served as witness, certifying Assistant, and Registrar in the 1733 Sue Squa land deed — three official roles in a single transaction.

narrative_blocks.header: He defended Tunxis land rights in court
narrative_blocks.body: In 1738, John Hooker provided a formal statement to the Connecticut General Court that ninety acres at Indian Neck had 'always belonged' to the Tunxis tribe — an unusual act of testimony on behalf of the indigenous people whose land his own ancestors had settled near.

narrative_blocks.header: The elm fell so someone could walk straight
narrative_blocks.body: His father gave him a site on the west side of Farmington's main street, where he built under the branches of a great elm. The tree became famous through all the region for the company that gathered beneath it — three generations of Hookers lived in its shade before a later owner cut it down to straighten the sidewalk.

narrative_blocks.header: The bell tolled — Hooker stepped out first
narrative_blocks.body: A chronicler recorded that every Sunday the bell rang, then began tolling; at the first stroke, Hooker issued from his house and proceeded to the Meeting House. The minister walked from his house. Whoever arrived first waited at the door; together they entered — at which the bell stopped and all the congregation arose.

narrative_blocks.header: The Sabbath ended when Hooker touched his gate
narrative_blocks.body: The holiness of Sunday was supposed to end at sunset — but no child in the town dared stir until John Hooker gave the signal. When the sun sank, he walked to his gate and leaned on it; open flew the doors and out came the children with a wild rush and shout.

narrative_blocks.header: Dark hair, dark eyes — dignity commanding respect
narrative_blocks.body: His grandson described him: "Dark hair and dark eyes, and a dignity in his air and expression that procured profound respect." A fine looking man, tall, very erect, and had a prominent large nose. He lived his entire life in Farmington, dying there in 1746 at age 81.

career.notes: Served as justice and register of Farmington
From Farmington. Served as Clerk two sessions; Speaker six sessions.
Served as judge of Superior Court concurrent with assistant role
Commissioner of the Mohegan's sequestered land at New London

cross_connections.display_label: Governor of New Haven and Connecticut colonies
was his father-in-law; son of Governor William Leete

narrative_blocks.header: He married the granddaughter of Governor William Leete
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Leete was the daughter of William Leete Jr. and the granddaughter of Governor William Leete — the only man to govern both New Haven Colony and the Colony of Connecticut. James Hooker, born a Farmington pastor's son, married into one of the most distinguished families in colonial Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: First Probate Judge — presided over the Assembly
narrative_blocks.body: He became the first Probate Judge of the Guilford district and repeatedly represented Guilford in the Colonial Assembly, serving for a number of sessions as its presiding officer. EH notes he "became a very prominent man at Guilford."

narrative_blocks.header: His only son died at 21, name extinct
narrative_blocks.body: EH notes he "had but one son who died unmarried, leaving no one to perpetuate his name." That son was William, Yale class of 1722, who died at 21.

career.notes: First judge of the Guilford probate district; also a town magistrate.
Repeatedly represented Guilford; presiding officer for several sessions.

bio.title: Honorable
bio.bio_blurb: First probate judge of the Guilford district

narrative_blocks.header: Betrothed at 29 — passed before the wedding
narrative_blocks.body: Roger Hooker was betrothed to Mary Standley of Hartford when he died on April 29, 1698, at age 29. Seven months later — December 28, 1698 — his brother Nathaniel married her instead.

naming_inspiration.description: Likely named for Rev. Roger Newton (I00003), his father's predecessor and brother-in-law at Farmington.

bio.bio_blurb: Grandson of Thomas Hooker; died unmarried at twenty-nine

cross_connections.display_label: His daughter married her son, founding lines merged.

narrative_blocks.header: His bride had been promised to his brother
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Standley had been betrothed to Nathaniel's brother Roger Hooker, who died April 29, 1698. Seven months later — December 28, 1698 — Nathaniel married her instead.

narrative_blocks.header: His wedding gift was half of Main Street
narrative_blocks.body: When Nathaniel married, his father-in-law Hon. Nathaniel Stanley gave him half his Hartford lot — extending from the Second Burial Ground to the little river. This became his place of business on the main street, just below the present Centre Church.

narrative_blocks.header: Two different Stanley families both married Hooker brothers
narrative_blocks.body: His brother John had married Abigail Standley of one Stanley family; Nathaniel married Mary Standley of a completely separate Stanley branch — Hon. Nathaniel Stanley of Hartford's line. Two unrelated families sharing the same name both converged on the Hooker brothers in the same generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Hartford's Colonial Assembly representative, dead at 40
narrative_blocks.body: He represented Hartford in the Connecticut Colonial Assembly for several years. He died November 21, 1711, at age 40, still in office.

narrative_blocks.header: His daughter married into the Talcott-Edwards line
narrative_blocks.body: His daughter Sarah Hooker married Hon. Daniel Edwards in 1728 — whose mother was Mary Talcott, daughter of Lt.-Col. John Talcott. Their children thus descend from both Rev. Thomas Hooker and the founding Talcott line.

bio.bio_blurb: Hartford colonial assemblyman; grandson of Thomas Hooker

cross_connections.display_label: fathered the New Haven minister she married in 1698
was mother to the New Haven minister she married in 1698
her granddaughter, last of the line the heirloom apron passed through

notable.notable_blurb: Wife of Yale co-founder Rev. James Pierpont

artworks.artwork_blurb: Companion portrait to husband Rev. James Pierpont, 1711; Yale Art Gallery.

narrative_blocks.header: Eight children in the house under the elms
narrative_blocks.body: Mary ran the New Haven parsonage through sixteen years of marriage and eight children, then presided over it as a widow for twenty-six more. The congregation had raised the house by voluntary subscription, and it stood for over a century.

narrative_blocks.header: Married James Pierpont the year her father died
narrative_blocks.body: Her father, the Farmington minister Samuel Hooker, died in November 1697; she married James Pierpont of New Haven the following year. The match carried her from one parsonage to the center of Connecticut’s emerging intellectual capital.

narrative_blocks.header: Her daughter’s courtship under the elms
narrative_blocks.body: It was Mary’s daughter Sarah whom Jonathan Edwards courted beneath the parsonage elms in 1726 — a match made in her widowed household that joined the Pierpont and Edwards lines and led on to college presidents and a vice president.

narrative_blocks.header: A son lost crossing the Connecticut
narrative_blocks.body: Her son Samuel — Yale 1718, newly ordained at Lyme — drowned in March 1723 crossing the Connecticut River in a canoe with an Indian guide. His body came ashore on Fishers Island six weeks later and was buried where it was found.

narrative_blocks.header: The family that named an artist
narrative_blocks.body: The unidentified colonial painter who took her portrait around 1711 is known to art history only as the Pierpont Limner — christened for this family, whose likenesses anchor his surviving work. Her husband’s companion portrait hangs at the Yale University Art Gallery.

narrative_blocks.header: A silk apron may trace to her line
narrative_blocks.body: A rare 1730s embroidered silk apron at the Connecticut Historical Society descends through three generations of Marys beginning with her. Though scholars doubt she stitched it herself, her advantageous marriage made such a luxury possible for the daughters who followed.

bio.photo_notes: Portrait of Mrs. James Pierpont (née Mary Hooker), 1711. Wikipedia Commons.

narrative_blocks.header: He died at Farmington at about age ten
narrative_blocks.body: Hezekiah Hooker was born at Farmington in 1675, one of Rev. Samuel Hooker's eleven children. He died there in 1686, about ten years old.

sources.full: Israel P. Warren D.D., The Stanley Families of America (1887)

notable.notable_blurb: Yale's first tutor and first B.A. graduate, 1703
notable.primary_url_label: Historic Buildings CT — Dr. Daniel Hooker House 1725

narrative_blocks.header: First Yale tutor, B.A. graduate in 1703
narrative_blocks.body: He was the first tutor appointed at the Collegiate School (Yale) by first rector Abraham Pierson Jr. — Yale's earliest faculty member. He received his Yale B.A. in 1703, alongside Rev. John Hart who was the first student to graduate.

narrative_blocks.header: Surgeon of the Canada expedition, 1711
narrative_blocks.body: He served as surgeon of the Massachusetts expedition against Canada in 1711 — the final major military operation of Queen Anne's War. He then returned to Wethersfield to practice medicine and law.

narrative_blocks.header: Declined his father's pulpit — chose medicine
narrative_blocks.body: After Rev. Samuel Hooker died in 1697, Farmington invited Daniel to succeed his father. He declined the pulpit and chose medicine — studying law alongside it, not to practice but to help patients make their wills.

narrative_blocks.header: First Farmington-born man with a college education
narrative_blocks.body: He graduated Harvard in 1700, then enrolled at the new Collegiate School in Saybrook to receive its B.A. in 1703. He was the first Farmington-born man to obtain a college education.

narrative_blocks.header: Inherited and divided Thomas Hooker's West Hartford farm
narrative_blocks.body: He inherited Thomas Hooker's farm in West Hartford and in 1728 deeded it to his sons. The land had been part of the original Hooker grant — passing through three generations from founder to surgeon.

institutions.institution_blurb: First Yale tutor 1701-1704; first B.A. graduate 1703.

cross_connections.display_label: He tutored under Pierson — Yale's first rector.
His niece married John Hart, his Yale successor
His successor as Yale tutor — second graduate
was his father-in-law

career.notes: First tutor of Yale Collegiate School. Hired by rector Abraham Pierson Jr. (X02127). Resigned in favor of Rev. John Hart (I00137).
Settled Wethersfield after leaving Yale. Also studied law to help patients make wills — no evidence he practiced law professionally.

education.notes: B.A. Harvard 1700. First Farmington-born man to receive a college education.

Received degree at Yale's first commencement 1703. EH says he was "the first B.A. of Yale" but also that Rev. John Hart (I00137) was "nominally the second, but really the first actual student to graduate." Daniel was the tutor/faculty; Hart was the first student B.A. Conflict flagged — see research_notes.

bio.title: Doctor
bio.bio_blurb: Yale's first tutor and first B.A. graduate; later a Wethersfield physician

burial.plot_notes: East Norwalk Historical Cemetery; no stone marks the grave (tradition). SOURCE: EH/FindAGrave.

narrative_blocks.header: The most accomplished lady Norwalk had ever seen
narrative_blocks.body: She was nicknamed 'Dame Buckingham' and described in family tradition as 'the most accomplished lady that had ever come to Norwalk.' She lived at Norwalk with her husband Rev. Stephen Buckingham from their 1703 marriage until her death in 1759.

narrative_blocks.header: No stone marks her grave — tradition knows
narrative_blocks.body: She died about March 26, 1759 in Norwalk, age 77. No marker survives at her grave; the location is preserved only in family tradition and her husband's burial records.

narrative_blocks.header: Her stepfather's son became her husband
narrative_blocks.body: After her father Rev. Samuel Hooker died in 1697, her mother Mary Willet married Rev. Thomas Buckingham of Saybrook. Thomas Buckingham was the father of Rev. Stephen Buckingham — whom Sarah herself married. After Thomas died in 1709, Mary came to Sarah and Stephen at Norwalk and lived with them until her death in 1712.

cross_connections.display_label: was her father-in-law

bio.bio_blurb: "Dame Buckingham" of Norwalk; granddaughter of Thomas Hooker

burialCemetery.name: Ancient Burying Ground
burialCemetery.notes: Adjacent to Center Church. Earliest surviving identifiable gravestone dates to 1648. Resting place of many Hartford founders including Rev. Thomas Hooker (exact location unknown).

INST006.name: Emmanuel College, Cambridge
INST006.notes: Founded by Sir Walter Mildmay. Thomas Hooker entered 1604, B.A. 1608, M.A. 1611, Fellow 1611-c.1618.

INST007.name: Market Bosworth Grammar School
INST007.notes: Thomas Hooker attended c. 1595-1604; approximately 10 miles from Tilton. Shuffelton's claim rests on Dixie fellowship restriction documented in Venn & Venn Alumni Cantabrigienses (1922).

crossConnections.display_label: was Hooker's deacon at Cambridge; followed to Hartford
neighbored Hooker's Cambridge circle in Hertfordshire
held the Cambridge lot adjoining Hooker's
married Hannah Holyoke, linking both founding families
co-wrote the Fundamental Orders; first Connecticut governor
served as the colony’s treasurer from 1641 until his death
